metadata.title: B2B Lead Generation: Signal-Based Outbound
metadata.description: Signal-based outbound: reach prospects based on buying signals, not cold lists. LinkedIn, email and phone in one automated system.

hero.label: / Outbound & Lead Generation
hero.title: Reach the right prospect, at the right moment.
hero.subtitle: Signal-based outbound: reach prospects based on buying signals, not cold lists. Automated, personalized, and working.

probleem.title: Cold outbound is dead. Smart outbound isn't.
paragraphs: The first customers came through your network. That worked, until it wasn't enough anymore. So you tried outbound: a list of 1,000 companies, generic emails, low response. It felt like spam — because it was. 95% of that list had no need at that moment.

The problem isn't outbound. The problem is timing and relevance. The companies that do grow with outbound do it differently: they reach prospects at the moment they show a buying signal. A new funding round. A sales hire. A technology switch. A visit to your website.
This is called signal-based outbound, and it's the difference between a 2% and 8% reply rate.

items.title: Signal Monitoring & Triggers
items.desc: Automatic tracking of buying signals: funding rounds, new hires, technology adoption, website visits and LinkedIn engagement. You only reach prospects who are signaling a need right now.

items.title: Outbound Email Engine
items.desc: Instantly or Lemlist, fully configured. Domain warmup, DKIM/SPF/DMARC, inbox rotation. Deliverability from day one. Every message triggered by a concrete buying signal.

items.title: LinkedIn Outreach
items.desc: Connection strategy and message sequences based on signals. Prospect just posted about a sales challenge? That's your opening — not a generic pitch.

items.title: Multi-Channel Sequences
items.desc: Email + LinkedIn + phone in one coordinated flow. Each step builds on the signal that triggered the sequence. No disconnected channels, one coordinated narrative.

items.title: AI Targeting & Personalization
items.desc: ICP-based lists via Clay and Apollo. AI enrichment combines 6+ data sources per prospect. Every outreach is personalized based on the specific signal — not based on a template.

items.title: Phone & Call Sequences
items.desc: Cold calling isn't dead, but it needs to be smart. Call scripts, call sequences, and conversation intelligence so every call gets better.

items.title: Define ICP & signals
items.desc: Who are your best-fit customers? Which buying signals are relevant? We define your ICP and the triggers.

items.title: Set up infra & monitoring
items.desc: Domains, tooling, warmup. Plus: Clay workflows that monitor funding, hiring and tech changes.

items.title: Build campaigns
items.desc: A campaign per signal type: different messaging for funding than for a sales hire. Relevance is everything.

items.title: Test, measure, optimize
items.desc: A/B tests on messaging and timing. Which signals convert best? Data-driven iteration.

voorJou: You depend on your network for new clients
You've tried outbound but it felt like spam
You want to reach prospects when they actually have a need
You want a predictable inflow of qualified leads

faq.a: Instead of a cold list of 1,000 companies, you only reach prospects who show a buying signal: a funding round, a new sales hire, a technology switch, or engagement with your content. The result: higher reply rates, more meetings, less spam.

faq.q: How many meetings can I expect?
faq.a: Depends on your market and offering. Typically 5-15 qualified meetings per month after the first optimization round. Signal-based outbound delivers on average 3-4x higher reply rates than traditional cold outbound.

faq.q: Isn't this just cold outbound with a different name?
faq.a: No. Cold outbound reaches everyone on a list. Signal-based outbound only reaches prospects who are signaling a need right now. The difference is in timing and relevance — and you see it directly in the results.

faq.q: How quickly is it up and running?
faq.a: Domain warmup takes 2-3 weeks. Signal monitoring can be running within a week. First results within 4-6 weeks. Optimization is ongoing.

items.title: Signal monitoring
items.desc: Clay automatically monitors funding rounds, hiring, tech changes and website visits. You know who's open right now, without manual searching.

items.title: AI personalization per signal
items.desc: Every message is written based on the specific buying signal. Prospect raised funding? Different messaging than someone hiring a sales manager.

items.title: Lead scoring on signal strength
items.desc: AI combines all signal types into one score. Multiple signals at once? That prospect goes to the top of your list.

cta.title: From cold lists to signal-based pipeline.
cta.desc: De Scan maps out which signals and channels deliver the most for your market.

hero.title: Why your first sales hire fails

blogSalesHire.intro: You have product-market fit. Revenue is growing. Time for that first sales hire. But 6 months later: no results, €100K+ spent, and you're back to selling yourself. I see this pattern in 80% of the founders I speak with.

pattern.title: The pattern
pattern.p1: You have product-market fit. Revenue is growing. You think:
pattern.p2: You hire someone. After 3-6 months: no results.

items.rest: , "Anyone who pays is a good customer"

pattern.conclusion: The sales hire doesn't fail due to lack of talent, but due to lack of system.

whatToBuild.title: What you need to build first
whatToBuild.intro: Before making that first sales hire, you need this in place:

items.title: 1. Defined ICP and buyer persona
items.desc: Who are your best customers? Which titles? Which company sizes? Which pain points?

items.title: 2. CRM set up with pipeline stages
items.desc: What steps does a deal go through? From first contact to closed-won. What is the definition of each stage?

items.title: 3. Discovery and demo scripts
items.desc: What questions do you ask? What do you show in your demo? How do you handle objections?

items.title: 4. Onboarding checklist
items.desc: What does someone need to learn in week 1, 2, 3, 4? Which tools? Which processes?

items.title: 5. First 3 months KPIs
items.desc: How many calls? How many demos? What is success in month 1, 2, 3?

solution.title: The solution
solution.p1: Build the system first, then the hire.
solution.p2: If you can't or don't want to do that yourself: let a Fractional Head of Sales lay the foundation. In 3-6 months you build a system where any sales hire can be successful.
solution.p3: Then you're not hiring a person who needs to figure it out. You're hiring someone who can execute a proven playbook.

metadata.title: Fractional Head of Sales: what is it and why is it growing in the Netherlands?
metadata.description: Everything about the Fractional Head of Sales model. What it is, what it costs, and why more and more B2B companies in the Netherlands are considering it.

hero.title: Fractional Head of Sales: what is it?

blogFractional.intro: In the US, it's been mainstream for years. In the Netherlands, it's just starting. A Fractional Head of Sales is an experienced commercial leader who works part-time (1-2 days per week) for your company. This article explains what it is, what it costs, and when it's smart.

whatIsIt.title: What is a Fractional Head of Sales?
whatIsIt.p1: A Fractional Head of Sales is an experienced sales leader who works part-time (usually 1-2 days per week) to build and lead your commercial strategy and organization.
whatIsIt.p2: Compare it to a fractional CFO or CMO: senior expertise, without the cost of a full-time hire.
whatIsIt.p3: In the US, more than 120,000 companies work with fractional executives. In Europe, the model is growing fast, especially at B2B tech companies between €1M-€10M revenue.
whatIsIt.boxTitle: What does a Fractional Head of Sales do?
boxItems: Builds your sales funnel and CRM process
Trains your team or first sales hire
Helps with hiring and onboarding sales talent

cost.title: What does it cost?

options.title: Interim sales manager
options.desc: 5 days per week, often short-term focus

cost.conclusion: Fractional is **60-70% cheaper** than full-time or interim, but you still get senior-level expertise.

whenSmart.title: When is it smart?
whenSmart.intro: A Fractional Head of Sales is smart if:
items: Your founder sells personally and wants to **systematize** it
You've just made your **first sales hire** (or that one failed)
You have **no sales system**: no CRM, no playbook, no pipeline
You want to **grow to €5M-€20M** but don't know how

whenNot.title: When is it NOT smart?
whenNot.intro: Fractional is not the right choice if:
items: You're under **€500K revenue**, focus on product-market fit first
You already have a **sales team of 5+**, then you need full-time leadership
You want someone who **will also sell**, fractional is strategic, not execution
You're not willing to **delegate authority**, a fractional needs to be able to decide

hero.title: What is signal-based outbound? The complete guide for B2B companies
hero.subtitle: Everything about signal-based selling: what it is, which buying signals exist, how to set it up, and why it's the logical next step for B2B companies.

intro.p1: You have a solid ICP. You have the tooling. You send hundreds of emails every week. And yet: 2% reply rate, a handful of meetings, and the feeling that you're spamming.
intro.p2: The problem isn't outbound. The problem is that you're reaching the wrong people at the wrong time. Signal-based outbound solves this. Instead of working through a cold list, you only approach prospects who show a buying signal — a sign that they're now open to what you offer.
intro.p3: The result: higher response rates, better conversations, and a pipeline that doesn't depend on luck. In this guide, I explain what signal-based outbound is, which signals exist, how to set it up, and why it's the logical next step for B2B companies.

traditional.title: Why traditional outbound keeps getting worse
traditional.p1: The traditional approach: build a list, write an email, start a sequence, and hope that a few of those 500-1,000 contacts respond. The problem is that of those 1,000 companies, maybe 3-5% have a relevant need at that moment.
traditional.p2: Decision-makers receive an average of 15 cold emails per week. 71% are ignored due to irrelevance. Google has lowered the spam threshold to 0.1% — meaning 1-2 complaints per 1,000 emails can damage your domain.
traditional.p3: The average reply rate on B2B cold emails has dropped to 4-5%. But the top 10% of outbound campaigns still achieve 8-12%. The difference? Timing and relevance. And that's exactly what signal-based outbound is about.

whatIs.title: What is signal-based outbound?
whatIs.p1: Instead of reaching everyone and hoping the timing is right, you monitor buying signals and only approach prospects who indicate they're open. A buying signal is any observable behavior or event that suggests a company currently has a need you can fill.
whatIs.p2: You go from 'cold calling 500 companies' to 'warm approaching 20 companies that are ready now.' That sounds like less volume, but it delivers more results — because every conversation is relevant.
whatIs.coldExample: Hi, I'm Tim from Accelr. We help B2B companies with their sales process. Would you be open to a quick chat?
whatIs.signalExample: I noticed you're looking for a Sales Manager. In my experience, that's often the moment when companies also review their sales process and tooling. Sound familiar?
whatIs.compareConclusion: That's the difference between a 2% and 8% reply rate.

signalTypes.title: The three types of buying signals
signalTypes.intro: Not all signals are equal. There are three categories, each with their own strength and reliability.
signalTypes.triggersTitle: 1. Trigger signals — events that create a need
signalTypes.triggersDesc: These are concrete, observable events that indicate a company is in motion. They're the strongest because they represent a factual change.
signalTypes.intentTitle: 2. Intent signals — behavior suggesting buying intent
signalTypes.intentDesc: These signals show that a company is actively exploring. Less concrete than triggers, but they provide a strong directional sense.
signalTypes.engagementTitle: 3. Engagement signals — interaction with your outreach
signalTypes.engagementDesc: These are reactions to your own communication. They confirm interest and indicate the right moment for a follow-up.

practice.title: What signal-based outbound looks like in practice
practice.intro: Let's make it concrete. This is what a typical signal-based workflow looks like:

steps.desc: You sharply define which companies you want to reach — not just firmographically (industry, size) but also on behavior (growth stage, tech stack, hiring patterns).
You configure Clay to automatically monitor triggers: new hires, funding rounds, tech switches. Leadinfo captures website visits. Sales Navigator tracks LinkedIn engagement.
A company in your ICP hires a new Head of Sales. Clay detects this automatically and enriches the contact with email, phone number and company data.
Clay's waterfall enrichment tries multiple data sources to find the best contact details. The result: a fully enriched profile, ready for outreach.
The enriched contact automatically goes to your outbound tool (Instantly, Lemlist) with a personalized message referencing the signal. Timing and relevance in one.

stack.title: The tech stack behind signal-based outbound
stack.intro: You don't need an enterprise budget. With the right combination of tools, you can set up signal-based outbound for less than €500 per month.
stack.conclusion: Total investment for a minimal stack: under €500 per month. That's a fraction of what traditional intent data providers like Bombora charge — and more relevant for the Dutch market.

whyNow.title: Why this is relevant now for B2B companies
whyNow.p1: Three developments make signal-based outbound accessible now. First: the tooling has become affordable. Clay, Apollo and Instantly were either unavailable or enterprise-only two years ago. Now you can run a complete stack for less than €500 per month.
whyNow.p2: Second: buyers expect relevance. B2B buyers do 70% of their research before they want to talk to sales. A generic message about 'having a quick chat' no longer works. Signal-based outbound gives you the context to be relevant from the first touchpoint.
whyNow.p3: Third: AI makes it scalable. Tools like Clay use AI to detect signals, enrich data and personalize messages. What used to require a team of 5 SDRs, you can now set up with 1-2 people — provided you have the right tools and processes.

pitfalls.title: The pitfalls of signal-based outbound
pitfalls.intro: Signal-based outbound is not a magic bullet. These are the most common mistakes:

items.title: Monitoring too many signals at once
items.desc: Start with 2-3 signals most relevant to your ICP. Only expand when you know which signals convert. More signals means more noise — not more results.

items.title: Using the signal as an excuse for a bad message
items.desc: A signal gives you the right to approach someone, but your message still needs to be relevant and valuable. 'I saw you raised funding, want a demo?' is not signal-based outbound — that's spam with an excuse.

items.title: Ignoring your own data
items.desc: Most companies sit on a mountain of first-party intent data they don't use. Who opens your emails? Who visits your website? Start there before investing in third-party tools.

items.title: Not building a feedback loop
items.desc: If you don't measure which signals actually lead to meetings and deals, you're optimizing on gut feeling. Build simple tracking per signal type: reply rate, meeting rate, pipeline value.

howToStart.title: How to get started
howToStart.intro: You don't need to set up everything at once. Here's a realistic timeline:

timeline.desc: Define your ICP sharply (firmographic + behavioral). Choose 2-3 signals. Create a Clay account and configure your first triggers.
Build your first campaigns per signal type. Start with one channel (email). Measure opens, replies and meetings.
Analyze which signals convert. Stop what doesn't work. Add a second channel (LinkedIn). Refine your messages based on data.
Automate what works. Add Leadinfo for website visits. Connect everything to your CRM. Scale with the signals that deliver proven results.

core.title: The core
core.p1: Signal-based outbound is about one fundamental shift: from 'who fits my ICP?' to 'who has a need right now?' That shift changes everything — your timing, your message, your conversion rate, and ultimately your pipeline.
core.p2: The technology is here. The tools are affordable. The only question is whether you're willing to stop volume-blasting and start listening to signals.

metadata.title: From cold outreach to signal-based outbound in 5 steps
metadata.description: How to switch from cold outreach to signal-based outbound in 5 concrete steps. With ICP framework, buying signals and campaign setup.

hero.title: From cold outreach to signal-based outbound in 5 steps

blogKoudeAcquisitie.intro: Cold outreach delivers less and less. The average reply rate on cold emails has dropped to 4-5% — and that number drops every year. Time for a different approach. In this article, I show you in 5 concrete steps how to switch to signal-based outbound.

whyWorse.title: Why cold outreach keeps getting worse
whyWorse.p1: The numbers don't lie. The average reply rate on B2B cold emails dropped from 8.5% in 2019 to 7% in 2023 and further to about 4-5% in 2025. Every year it gets worse, and there are three hard reasons.
whyWorse.p2: **Overflowing inboxes.** Decision-makers receive an average of 15 cold emails per week. 71% are ignored due to irrelevance. Your competitor sends the same template to the same list — and you're drowning together in the inbox.
whyWorse.p3: **Stricter spam filters.** Google has lowered the spam threshold to 0.1%. That means: 1-2 complaints per 1,000 emails and your domain is in trouble. Apple's Mail Privacy Protection and Outlook's new filtering don't help either.
whyWorse.p4: **Buyer behavior has changed.** B2B buyers do 70% of their research before they want to talk to sales. They expect relevance. A generic message about 'having a quick chat' no longer works — it irritates.
whyWorse.p5: The top 10% of outbound campaigns still achieve 8-12% reply rates. The difference? Timing and relevance. And that's exactly what signal-based outbound is about.

whatIs.title: What is signal-based outbound?
whatIs.p1: Instead of buying a list and sending everyone the same message, you flip it: you wait for a signal indicating someone is open to your solution, and only then do you reach out.
whatIs.p2: Those signals — also called buying signals or buyer signals — are concrete, observable events: a company raises funding, hires a new sales manager, visits your website, or switches technology. When you respond to something that just happened, your outreach doesn't feel like spam but like relevant help.

step1.title: Step 1: Define your ICP more sharply
step1.intro: Most companies have an ICP that's too broad. 'B2B SaaS companies with 50-500 employees' is not an ICP — it's a market. For signal-based outbound you need to be sharper, because you'll build campaigns per signal type.
step1.firmographic: **Firmographic:** revenue, number of employees, industry, location, growth stage. This is the foundation you probably already have.
step1.behavioral: **Behavioral:** this is where most companies fall short. Add: what technology do they use? Are they growing? Are they investing in sales and marketing?
items: **IT service providers:** look for companies migrating to cloud, implementing new security tooling, or expanding their IT team
**SaaS companies:** focus on companies that just raised Series A/B, are appointing a new CRO, or doubling their sales team
**Consultancy:** target companies with a new strategic project (merger, new market, digital transformation)
step1.tip: Write out your ICP in maximum 5 sentences. If you can't describe it in 5 sentences, it's too broad.
step1.example: Example: 'We help Dutch B2B SaaS companies between 20-100 employees that just raised Series A, are actively expanding their sales team, and use HubSpot as CRM.'

step2.title: Step 2: Choose 3-5 relevant buying signals
step2.intro: You don't need to monitor all signals at once. Start with 3-5 signals most relevant to your business.

items: New sales or marketing hire (signals growth ambition)
Technology adoption or switch (they're evaluating alternatives)
New CEO or CRO appointed (new leadership = new priorities)
New market or office opened (they need help scaling)
Prospect opens your emails repeatedly (warm interest)
LinkedIn engagement with your content (they already know you)

step2.tip: The rule of thumb: choose signals where you can actually attach a relevant opening line.

step3.title: Step 3: Set up your monitoring
step3.intro: Now it gets technical — but not complicated. You need three layers.
step3.layer1Desc: Clay is the hub of your signal-based setup. You build 'tables' that automatically monitor triggers: new hires on LinkedIn, funding rounds via Crunchbase, technology changes via BuiltWith. Clay checks 150+ data sources and automatically enriches with company and contact data.
step3.layer2Desc: Leadinfo (Dutch, GDPR-compliant) shows which companies visit your website. Not who exactly, but which company, which pages, how often. Connect this to your ICP filter and you have a daily list of warm leads.
step3.layer3Desc: LinkedIn Sales Navigator (Advanced edition) has buyer intent built in. You see which contacts at your saved accounts visit your company page, like your posts, or accept your InMails.
step3.conclusion: The combination of these three — Clay for events, Leadinfo for website visits, Sales Nav for LinkedIn engagement — gives you a complete picture of which companies are ready now.

step4.title: Step 4: Build campaigns per signal type
step4.intro: This is where most teams make a mistake: they capture all signals in one generic outbound campaign. Don't do that. Each signal type deserves its own message and cadence.

examples.opening: Saw that you just raised funding — congratulations. At similar scale-ups in your industry, we see that the sales process is often the first bottleneck when scaling.
examples.cadence: 1 email, 1 LinkedIn message, 1 follow-up after 5 days

examples.channel: Email (personal, not 'I saw you were on our site')
examples.opening: We help B2B companies in your industry set up a scalable sales process. Is this something that's on your radar right now?

examples.timing: 2-4 weeks after start date (let them settle in)
examples.opening: Congratulations on your new role. Other sales leaders in your industry use signal-based outbound to deliver results faster — sound familiar?
examples.cadence: LinkedIn connection, after acceptance 1 message, 1 follow-up email

step4.tip: Use a tool like Instantly or Lemlist for execution. Both integrate with Clay, so you can load enriched data directly into your campaigns.

step5.title: Step 5: Measure and optimize
step5.intro: Signal-based outbound is not 'set and forget.' You need to measure which signals actually convert — not just to replies, but to meetings and deals.
discoveries: 1-2 of your 5 signals deliver 80% of results
Timing makes more difference than copy
The combination of signals (e.g., website visit + new hire) is stronger than individual signals
optimize: **Signal selection:** stop with signals that don't convert
**Timing:** test whether responding faster makes a difference
**Copy:** A/B test opening lines per signal type
**Channel:** test email vs. LinkedIn vs. multichannel per signal
step5.reviewTip: Review your data every 2 weeks. After 3 months you have enough data to streamline your setup to the 2-3 signals that truly work for your business.
step5.compareTip: Always compare with your old approach. If your cold outbound had a 3% reply rate and your signal-based campaign is at 12%, you know the extra monitoring time is worth it.

conclusion.title: Conclusion: start with 1 signal type
conclusion.intro: You don't need to set up everything at once. The best way to start:
steps: **Choose 1 signal** that's most logical for your ICP
**Set up monitoring** via Clay or Leadinfo
**Build 1 campaign** with a specific opening line
conclusion.closing: The difference between cold outreach and signal-based outbound isn't the technology — it's the mindset. You stop sending to everyone and start listening to who is ready.

metadata.title: 10 buying signals you can use tomorrow
metadata.description: The 10 most powerful buyer signals for B2B outbound, with detection method and example opening line per signal.

hero.title: 10 buying signals you can use tomorrow for B2B outbound

blogKoopsignalen.intro: You don't have to guess who's ready to buy. Buying signals tell you exactly which companies are now open to a conversation. In this article: 10 concrete buyer signals, how to detect them, and an example opening line per signal.

why.title: Why buying signals change your outbound
why.p1: The difference between a cold email with 3% reply rate and one with 15%? Timing and relevance. Buying signals give you both. Instead of blindly working through a list, you respond to something concrete that just happened.
why.p2: Below are the 10 most powerful signals, each with: what it is, why it works, how to detect it, and a ready-to-use opening line.

signals.title: Funding round raised
signals.what: A company raises seed, Series A, B or growth capital.
signals.why: There's fresh budget, they're scaling up, and decisions need to be made quickly. Companies that just raised funding actively invest in tooling, processes and people.
signals.how: Clay automatically monitors funding rounds via Crunchbase integration. Set a trigger for companies in your ICP that raised funding in the past 30 days.
signals.opening: Congratulations on the funding round. At similar scale-ups in your industry, we see that the sales process becomes the first bottleneck when scaling — does that sound familiar?

signals.title: New sales or marketing hire
signals.what: A company hires a new sales or marketing manager, director or VP.
signals.why: New hires want to prove themselves quickly. They evaluate existing tooling, bring new ideas, and have budget to invest. The first 90 days are the window when they're most open.
signals.how: Clay tracks new hires via LinkedIn data. Filter on job titles like 'Head of Sales', 'Marketing Manager', 'VP Revenue' at companies in your ICP.
signals.opening: Saw that you just started as [title] at [company] — congratulations. Other sales leaders in your industry use signal-based outbound to deliver results faster.

signals.title: New CEO or CRO appointed
signals.what: There's a C-level change, specifically CEO, CRO or COO.
signals.why: New leadership means new strategy. A new CEO or CRO re-evaluates existing vendors, processes and priorities. This is when budgets shift.
signals.how: LinkedIn (manually or via Clay). Set alerts on companies in your ICP for C-level changes.
signals.opening: With a new CRO at the helm, a lot often changes in a short time. At similar companies, we helped the new leadership team streamline their sales process within 6 months.

signals.title: Company visits your website
signals.what: A company visits your website — specifically pages like pricing, case studies or product pages.
signals.why: They're actively exploring. This is first-party intent data — the strongest signal there is, because they're already looking for you.
signals.how: Leadinfo is the go-to for Dutch companies. It identifies 35-40% of B2B traffic in the Benelux. Cookieless, GDPR-compliant, and from €69/month.
signals.opening: We help B2B companies set up a scalable sales process. Given your focus on growth — is this something that's on your radar right now?
signals.note: Note: never refer to the website visit itself in your message. That feels creepy. Use the signal to determine your timing, not as a conversation opener.

signals.title: Technology adoption or switch
signals.what: A company implements new software or switches from one tool to another.
signals.why: Technology switches mean active evaluation is happening. If a company switches from tool A to tool B, there's a good chance they're also reconsidering adjacent tools.
signals.how: Clay integrates with BuiltWith for technographic data. You can set triggers on specific technology adoptions or switches.
signals.opening: Noticed you recently started using a new tool. Companies making this move often run into specific challenges — we help solve those.

signals.title: LinkedIn engagement with your content
signals.what: A prospect likes, shares or comments on your LinkedIn posts or company page.
signals.why: They already know you. There's brand awareness and an initial form of trust. A connection request or DM therefore doesn't feel cold but like a logical next step.
signals.how: LinkedIn Sales Navigator (Advanced) shows engagement with your content per saved lead and account.
signals.opening: Thanks for your reaction on my post about signal-based outbound. I'm curious — is this something that's relevant at your company?

signals.title: Searching relevant keywords (intent data)
signals.what: A company actively searches for topics related to your solution.
signals.why: They're actively in an exploration phase. This is classic third-party intent data.
signals.how: Bombora is the market leader but costs $30,000-$100,000 per year and is primarily US-focused. For Dutch SMBs, use Google Search Console + Leadinfo as a free alternative.
signals.opening: I noticed this topic is high on your agenda. At similar companies, we helped streamline their sales process — would it be worth a quick chat?

signals.title: Negative reviews about internal culture
signals.what: A company gets negative Glassdoor reviews about sales culture, high turnover, or lack of tooling.
signals.why: This signals internal problems that your solution might address. High sales turnover? Maybe proper tooling or training is missing.
signals.how: Manually monitor Glassdoor at companies in your ICP. Clay can scrape Glassdoor data and filter on relevant keywords.
signals.opening: I work a lot with sales teams in your industry that struggle with structuring their sales process. Sound familiar?
signals.note: Note: never refer to the Glassdoor reviews themselves. Use the insight, not the source.

signals.title: New market or office
signals.what: A company opens a new office, enters a new market, or launches in a new country.
signals.why: Growth into new markets creates direct needs: local lead generation, new processes, partnerships, and market knowledge.
signals.how: Chamber of Commerce mutations for Dutch companies. LinkedIn company pages for international expansion. Clay can monitor company updates.
signals.opening: Congratulations on the move to the new market. Other companies in your industry that made the same move ran into specific sales challenges — something you're dealing with too?

signals.title: Prospect opens your emails repeatedly
signals.what: A prospect opens the same email 3+ times, or opens multiple emails in your sequence.
signals.why: Repeated opening means interest. They're hesitating, sharing internally, or waiting for the right moment.
signals.how: Email tools like Instantly, Lemlist or HubSpot track opens. Filter on prospects who open 3+ times but don't respond.
signals.opening: I wanted to check if my previous email was relevant — I understand the timing isn't always right. Is the sales process something that's relevant now or later?

howToStart.title: How to get started
howToStart.p1: You don't need to monitor all 10 signals at once. Choose 2-3 that are most relevant to your ICP and industry, and build campaigns around them.
howToStart.p2: The easiest start: **signal 4 (website visits via Leadinfo)** and **signal 2 (new hires via Clay)**. Both are affordable, easy to set up, and deliver immediate results.

hero.title: Clay, Apollo, Leadinfo: the tools behind signal-based outbound

blogTools.intro: You know what signal-based outbound is and which buying signals you can use. But which tools do you need? In this article, I compare the most important tools per category — with current pricing, tips and pitfalls.
blogTools.categoriesIntro: Signal-based outbound isn't about one tool but a stack. You need five layers: signal detection, prospecting data, website visitor identification, outbound execution and orchestration.

tools.categoryDesc: The hub of your signal-based setup. This is where you detect buying signals and enrich contact data.

entries.name: Clay
entries.what: Clay monitors buying signals (new hires, funding, tech switches), enriches contact data via 150+ data sources, and routes that data to your outbound tools. Clay's 'waterfall enrichment' automatically tries multiple data sources if the first one doesn't return results.
entries.forWho: B2B teams serious about scaling with signal-based outbound. Clay requires a 1-2 week learning curve, but is a force multiplier after that.
entries.tip: Start with the Explorer tier. The Starter doesn't have the AI formula generator, which you need for custom enrichments.
entries.pitfall: Credits run out fast if you filter too broadly. Start with a specific ICP segment and only expand when you know which signals convert.

tools.categoryDesc: Contact data and LinkedIn intelligence. The foundation for your outreach.

entries.name: Apollo
entries.what: Apollo combines a database of 275+ million contacts with built-in sequencing. You can search, filter, enrich and email directly from one platform.
entries.forWho: Founders and small sales teams wanting an all-in-one for prospecting + outreach. Apollo's free tier is the best free option on the market.
entries.tip: Use Apollo for data and combine it with Clay for signal detection. Apollo's built-in sequencing is fine for starters.
entries.pitfall: Mobile numbers and export credits are separately limited. Check how many you need monthly before choosing a tier.

entries.name: LinkedIn Sales Navigator
entries.what: Advanced search in LinkedIn's database, saved leads with alerts, and (in the Advanced edition) buyer intent signals.
pricing: Advanced Plus: custom pricing, from ~$1,600/year per seat
entries.forWho: Everyone doing B2B outbound. Sales Nav isn't optional — it's essential. The Core edition suffices for most.
entries.tip: Combine Sales Nav with Clay. Clay can pull and enrich LinkedIn data, giving you the best of both worlds.
entries.pitfall: Watch out for VAT — it's on top of the listed prices and can be 21% extra. InMail credits (50/month on Core) run out fast.

tools.categoryDesc: Who visits your website? This is first-party intent data in its purest form.

entries.name: Leadinfo
entries.what: Identifies which companies visit your website. Dutch, GDPR-compliant, cookieless. Identifies 35-40% of B2B traffic in the Benelux — the highest in the EU market.
entries.tip: Connect Leadinfo directly to your CRM (HubSpot, Salesforce) so website visits automatically land at the right account.
entries.pitfall: Leadinfo shows companies, not individuals. You need to find the right contact person yourself — that's where Clay or Apollo comes in.

entries.name: Leadfeeder (via Dealfront)
entries.what: Similar to Leadinfo, but with broader international coverage via Dealfront's data ecosystem.
entries.forWho: Teams with international clients outside the Benelux.
entries.tip: For NL focus → Leadinfo. For international → Leadfeeder. Running both simultaneously is rarely needed.
entries.pitfall: Website visit data is only valuable when you have enough traffic. Below 500 unique visitors per month, it yields too few usable leads.

tools.categoryDesc: The tools for sending your messages. Email, LinkedIn, multichannel.

entries.name: Instantly
entries.what: Cold email at scale with unlimited mailboxes and built-in warmup. The best value for pure email outbound.
entries.tip: Instantly is NOT a prospecting tool. Connect it to Clay or Apollo for your data. Instantly does one thing well: sending emails with high deliverability.
entries.pitfall: Lead credits for the built-in database cost extra. Use Apollo or Clay for data, Instantly only for sending.

entries.name: Lemlist
entries.what: Multichannel outreach: email + LinkedIn + calls, with AI personalization.
pricing: Multichannel Expert: $79-99/user/month — LinkedIn automation, calls, AI
entries.forWho: Teams wanting multichannel from one tool. Lemlist's LinkedIn automation is a plus compared to Instantly.
entries.tip: Lemlist is strong in multichannel. If you only need email, Instantly is cheaper.
entries.pitfall: Per-user pricing adds up with larger teams. Credits expire monthly without rollover.

entries.name: La Growth Machine (LGM)
entries.what: Multichannel sequences via LinkedIn, email and X (Twitter), with visual workflow builder. Popular in the European market.
pricing: Basic: €60/month per identity — LinkedIn + email, 3 campaigns
Pro: €120/month per identity — + calls, A/B testing
entries.forWho: Teams wanting visual workflows and X as a channel.
entries.tip: LGM is strong in visual campaign builders. Good for teams wanting to build processes visually.
entries.pitfall: LGM charges per 'identity' (1 LinkedIn + 1 email). With multiple SDRs, that adds up quickly.

tools.categoryDesc: The glue between your tools. Connects Clay, your CRM, your outbound tool and your signal monitoring into one workflow.

entries.name: n8n
entries.what: Open-source workflow automation. Self-hosted or cloud. Powerful but more technical than Make.
entries.tip: n8n is the most powerful option if you can self-host. Full control over your data and workflows.
entries.pitfall: Requires technical knowledge. Not suitable if you don't have a developer on the team.

entries.name: Make (formerly Integromat)
entries.what: Visual workflow builder. More user-friendly than n8n, with a large ecosystem of integrations.
entries.forWho: Non-technical teams needing to connect tools without coding.
entries.tip: Start with Make if you need to connect tools that don't have native integrations. The visual builder is intuitive.
entries.pitfall: Complex workflows can quickly consume credits. Monitor your usage.

entries.name: HubSpot (Free CRM)
entries.what: Free CRM with workflows, contact management and basic automation. The free tier is surprisingly complete for small teams.
entries.forWho: Everyone. HubSpot Free is the best free CRM for B2B.
entries.tip: Start with HubSpot Free as CRM. Add Make if you need to connect tools without native integrations.
entries.pitfall: The jump from Free to Professional is large (€0 → €890/month). Plan ahead which features you truly need.

minimalStack.title: The minimal stack: under €500/month
minimalStack.intro: For a founder or small team wanting to start with signal-based outbound:
minimalStack.conclusion: This gives you signal detection, data enrichment, email outbound and a CRM. Enough to start and validate which signals work.

premiumStack.title: The premium stack: under €1,000/month
premiumStack.intro: When you know signal-based outbound works and want to scale:
premiumStack.conclusion: Well under €1,000, and you have a complete stack for signal detection, prospecting, website visits, multichannel outreach and automation.

dontStartAll.title: Don't start with everything at once
dontStartAll.p1: The pitfall is buying all tools at once and then using 20% of them. Start with the minimal stack, validate your first signal type, and only add tools when you know what works.
dontStartAll.p2: The most successful signal-based teams I know started with Clay + one outbound tool. After 2-3 months they knew which signals converted, and only then did they expand. That order saves you hundreds of euros on tools you don't need yet.

metadata.title: Intent data for Dutch SMBs: what actually works?
metadata.description: Honest overview of intent data tools: what works for Dutch SMBs and what doesn't. Bombora, 6sense, G2 vs. Leadinfo, Clay, LinkedIn.

hero.title: Intent data for Dutch SMBs: what actually works?

blogIntentData.intro: Most content about intent data is written for American enterprise companies with six- to seven-figure marketing budgets. Not this article. Here you'll read what intent data does and doesn't deliver for Dutch SMBs — honestly, without a sales pitch.

whatIs.title: What is intent data?
whatIs.p1: Intent data is information indicating that a company is actively interested in a specific topic, product or solution. The idea: if you know a company is exploring your category, you can approach them before they end up at the competitor.
whatIs.firstPartyDesc: Behavior you measure yourself: website visits, email opens, content downloads, demo requests. This is data you already have (or can easily collect) via your own website and tools.
whatIs.thirdPartyDesc: Behavior that external parties measure: which topics a company researches on the broader web, which reviews they read, which comparison pages they visit. Providers like Bombora, 6sense and G2 sell this data.
whatIs.p3: The reliability difference is large. First-party data is concrete — someone visits your pricing page, that's a hard signal. Third-party data is a proxy — a company reads an article about 'CRM implementation' somewhere, and that gets translated into an intent score.

doesntWork.title: What DOESN'T work for Dutch SMBs
doesntWork.intro: Here it gets uncomfortable. The three biggest names in intent data are simply not relevant for most Dutch SMBs.

items.name: Bombora
items.desc: Market leader in third-party intent data. Monitors content consumption on 5,000+ B2B websites and translates that into 'Company Surge' scores per topic.
items.problem: Starts at $30,000/year, average contract ~$58,000/year. Annual contract required. The network is primarily American — overlap with Dutch B2B websites is minimal.

items.name: 6sense
items.desc: Combines intent data with predictive analytics and account-based marketing. Powerful platform — for enterprise.
items.problem: Median customer pays $55,000/year. Enterprise up to $250,000-$400,000/year excluding implementation costs ($10,000-$50,000). Requires a dedicated RevOps person.

items.name: G2 intent data
items.desc: Buyer intent data based on who views your product page, reviews and comparisons on G2.com.
items.problem: Dutch B2B buyers barely use G2. The platform is dominant in North America. Signal volumes from the Netherlands are too low for usable data.

doesWork.title: What DOES work for Dutch SMBs
doesWork.intro: The good news: there are alternatives that better fit the Dutch market, and they cost a fraction of the above options.

items.name: Leadinfo: website visits as first-party intent
items.desc: Leadinfo identifies which companies visit your website — and that's the most direct form of intent data there is. Dutch, GDPR-compliant, cookieless, 35-40% identification of B2B traffic in the Benelux. From €69/month, no annual contract.
items.why: You see which company, which pages, how often, and how long. Filter on your ICP and you have a daily list of warm leads.

items.name: LinkedIn Sales Navigator: buyer intent built in
items.desc: Sales Navigator Advanced contains buyer intent signals: which contacts at your saved accounts visit your company page, like your content, or accept InMails. With 180+ signals it builds an intent score per account. From $139.99/month (annual).
items.why: Since virtually every Dutch B2B company is active on LinkedIn, the data sample here IS large enough.

items.name: Clay triggers: event-based intent
items.desc: Clay monitors concrete events that signal intent: new hires, funding rounds, technology switches, press releases. This isn't a statistical intent score but an observable fact. Clay Explorer costs $314/month.
items.why: You don't need to interpret a score. There's no 'medium intent' label — you see that company X hired a VP Sales last week. Concrete, verifiable, and immediately actionable.

items.name: Own first-party data via HubSpot
items.desc: HubSpot (free CRM) tracks who opens your emails, which pages they visit (with tracking code), and how they move through your funnel. This is first-party intent data you already have but probably don't use enough.
items.why: Combine HubSpot data with Leadinfo and you have a complete picture: which companies visit your site (Leadinfo) and what do known contacts do (HubSpot). Cost: €69/month + €0.

truth.title: The honest truth
truth.p1: The intent data industry is built on the promise that you can know who's ready to buy. For American enterprise companies with huge target markets and six-figure budgets, that model works. For Dutch SMBs, the reality is different.
truth.highlight: First-party data + event-based triggers > expensive third-party intent data.
truth.p2: Your own website visit data (Leadinfo) tells you more than a Bombora Company Surge score ever will for the Dutch market. LinkedIn engagement from your target audience is a stronger signal than a G2 comparison page your prospect never visited.
truth.p3: The companies performing best with signal-based outbound in the Netherlands don't use Bombora or 6sense. They combine Leadinfo + Clay + LinkedIn Sales Nav + their own HubSpot data. Total cost: €400-600/month. That's 10x cheaper than Bombora's entry price — and more relevant for the Dutch market.

startWith.title: Start with what you have
startWith.intro: Before you invest in any intent data tool, do this:
startWith.conclusion: Most SMBs discover they're already sitting on a mountain of intent data they're not using. Start there.
startWith.closing: And when you notice that foundation works — that website visit data actually leads to meetings, that email engagement correlates with deals — only then is it time to invest in Clay for scale and automation. Not sooner.

startSteps: **Check your HubSpot** (or other CRM): who opens your emails, who visits your site, who's in your pipeline but has gone quiet?
**Install Leadinfo** (30-day free trial): see how many companies visit your website that fit your ICP
**Use LinkedIn Sales Nav** to see who's engaged with your content
**Only then** consider Clay for automated signal detection
